elements.sent-2411-001: Physical Parameters and Feedback of Massive Stars in N11 B \and {L.~M.. Oskinova\inst{\ref{UP \and {W.-R.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Wofford\inst{\ref{IA \institute{ {Institut f{\"u r Physik und Astronomie, Universit{\"a t Potsdam, Karl-Liebknecht-Str.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Tijuana–Ensenada, Ensenada, BC 22860, Mexico \abstract { Massive stars lead the ionization and mechanical feedback within young star-forming regions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) is an ideal galaxy for studying individual massive stars and quantifying their feedback contribution to the environment.
elements.sent-2411-016: We analyze eight exemplary targets in LMC N11\,B from the Hubble UV Legacy Library of Young Stars as Essential Standards (ULLYSES) program, using novel spectra from \hst\ (\cosi\ and \stis) in the UV, and from VLT (\xshooter) in the optical.
elements.sent-2411-017: We model the spectra of early to late O-type stars by using state-of-the-art PoWR atmosphere models.
elements.sent-2411-018: We determine the stellar and wind parameters (e.g., \tstar, \logg, $L_{\star}$, $\dot{M}$, ${\varv _{\infty}$) of the analyzed objects, chemical abundances (C, N, O), ionizing and mechanical feedback (\QH, \QHeI, \QHeII, \lmec) and X-rays.}
elements.sent-2411-019: We report ages of $2-4.5$~Myr and masses of $30-60$~\Msun\ for the analyzed stars in N11\,B, consistent with a scenario of sequential star formation.
elements.sent-2411-020: We note that the observed wind-momentum luminosity relation is consistent with theoretical predictions.
elements.sent-2411-021: We detect nitrogen enrichment in most of the stars, up to a factor of seven.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, we do not find a correlation between nitrogen enrichment and projected rotational velocity.
elements.sent-2411-023: Finally, based on their spectral type, we estimate the total ionizing photons injected from the O-type stars in N11\,B into its environment.
elements.sent-2411-024: We report $\log$ ($\sum$\,\QH)$=50.5$~ph\,s$^{-1}$, $\log$ ($\sum$\,\QHeI)$=49.6$~ph\,s$^{-1}$ and $\log$ ($\sum$\,\QHeII)$=44.4$~ph\,s$^{-1}$, consistent with the total ionizing budget in N11.
elements.sent-2411-025: Massive stars, defined as those with initial masses ($M_\mathrm{i}$) on the main sequence $\geq8$~M$_{\odot}$, are key to understanding multiple astrophysical phenomena: from fundamental nucleosynthesis processes (e.g., CNO cycle and He-burning) to interstellar medium (ISM) feedback; such as the injection of mechanical energy, ionizing photons, and fresh elements into their local environments.
elements.sent-2411-026: Thus, massive stars are expected to have a significant impact on the evolution of their host galaxies \citep[see][and references therein]{Massey2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, understanding the individual contribution of massive stars to the total feedback of their local environments is still ongoing, and proper quantification is still needed.
elements.sent-2411-028: In order to address this issue, a comprehensive analysis of the stellar and wind parameters, as well as chemical abundances of these objects, must include not only the optical wavelengths, but specially the ultra-violet (UV) range of the spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-029: It is known that most of the luminosity and crucial wind features of massive stars are precisely in the UV wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-030: The analysis of multi-wavelength spectral observations, complemented with precise photometry, together with state-of-the-art stellar atmosphere models, is critical for establishing the feedback of individual massive stars and investigating the ecology of their environments, from local to larger scales.
elements.sent-2411-031: Stars even more massive ($M_\mathrm{i}\geq20-25$~M$_{\odot}$), therefore hotter, more luminous, and younger on the main sequence -- the O-type stars -- are particularly important \citep[see e.g.,][]{Langer2012 as they lead to some of the most exotic phenomena in their last stages of evolution, such as: the classical Wolf-Rayet (WR) stars \citep[][]{Crowther2007 , core-collapse supernovae (SNe) of type Ibc \citep[][]{Woosley2005 , long-duration gamma-ray bursts (GRB) \citep[][]{Woosley2006 , compact objects such as neutron stars and black holes \citep[][]{Heger2003 , and gravitational-wave sources \citep[][]{Abbott2017 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: In order to understand the role that massive stars played in the first galaxies, now being observed at higher redshifts with present-generation facilities such as the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) \citep[e.g.,][]{Arellano2022 , we can use lower-than-solar metallicity environments as local proxy scenarios to study the astrophysical processes and feedback mechanisms occurring in earlier stages of the Universe, after the reionization epoch to the present \citep[][]{Wofford2021, Eldridge2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) is known to have an average metallicity of around half-solar \citep[$\sim$0.5~\zsol;][]{Larsen2000,Hunter2007 , with no apparent metallicity gradient \citep[e.g.,][]{Dominguez2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Although its metal deficiency is modest with respect to extreme metal-poor galaxies \citep[e.g., I Zw\,18; 12$+\log$(O/H)$\leq7.2$;][at 13.4~Mpc]{Izotov2019 , its nearby distance \citep[DM=18.5~mag (50~kpc);][]{Pietrzynski2013 and its relatively low reddening \citep[\ebv$=0.05$~mag;][]{Larsen2000 make it an ideal place to study individual massive stars in great detail.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Certainly, the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) has an even lower metallicity ($\sim$0.2~\zsun), and individual stars are being subject of recent studies \citep[e.g.,][]{Pauli2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, a comprehensive understanding of massive stars at any metallicity must include the O-type stars in the LMC as a reference frame for comparative analysis.
elements.sent-2411-037: Additionally, there are not many "nearby-enough" systems containing massive stars with the adequate observations (e.g., dedicated spectra with the proper signal-to-noise ratios (SNR), spatial resolution and resolving power, wavelength coverage and precise photometry) for the proper characterization of their main physical parameters with modern stellar atmosphere models.
elements.sent-2411-038: In the LMC, N11 is the second-brightest star-forming region, just after the well-known 30 Doradus (30 Dor) \citep[see][]{Pellegrini2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Here we focus our study in N11\,B \citep[][]{Henize1956 , a.k.a.}
elements.sent-2411-040: LH\,10, the brightest \hii\ region of N11.
elements.sent-2411-041: N11\,B is located in the periphery of LH\,9 \citep[][]{Lucke1970 , a gas-depleted cavity of $\sim$100~pc in diameter at the center of N11.}
elements.sent-2411-042: N11\,B contains a rich population of massive stars, with at least 25 known O-type stars, and 9 B-type stars, from a catalog complete at V$=16$~mag \citep[][]{Parker1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: General parameters of the region are provided in Tab.~\ref{tab:parameters .}
elements.sent-2411-044: In Fig.~\ref{fig:n11b-hst , we show the known blue and bright stars located in N11\,B.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Additionally, there is only one SN remnant observed by XMM Newton close to N11.
elements.sent-2411-046: However, given its projected distance from N11\,B, it is unlikely to be associated with this star-forming region, where no SNe have been reported so far, neither any resolved (point-like) source of X-ray emission.
elements.sent-2411-047: Given the brightness of this region, N11 has been the subject of multiple studies.
elements.sent-2411-048: Based on its spectral type and metallicity, \citet[][in their Fig. 12]{Evans2006 adopted a temperature for the stars, and the luminosities were determined based on their colours and distances.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Later, \citet[][]{Mokiem2007 analyzed 6~O-type stars in N11\,B from \citet[][]{Evans2006 (IDs: N11\,31, 32, 38, 48 and 60), excluding the known binaries (see Fig.~\ref{fig:n11b-hst ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: These studies were done in the optical range.

elements.sent-2411-051: UV spectra of massive stars are essential for studying their wind parameters.
elements.sent-2411-052: These include not only the wind mass-loss rate and terminal wind velocity, but also chemical abundances and the presence (or absence) of X-rays.
elements.sent-2411-053: It is for this reason that the Hubble Space Telescope's (\hst) Ultraviolet Legacy Library of Young Stars as Essential Standards (ULLYSES) program \citep[][]{Roman-Duval2020 dedicated 1000~\hst\ orbits in order to construct a UV spectroscopic library of young high- and low-mass stars in the local universe.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Eight O-type stars of N11\,B are ULLYSES targets \citep[see][]{Vink2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: In addition, these objects also include optical spectra, obtained from Very Large Telescope (VLT) \xshooter\ and \giraffe\ instruments.
elements.sent-2411-056: On this basis, by using dedicated multi-wavelength spectra, as well as photometry in the UV, optical (including recent Gaia DR3), and near-infrared (NIR), we perform a detailed study of a sample of massive stars in the star-forming complex N11\,B.

elements.sent-2411-057: We use novel observations of eight O-type stars in N11\,B to model their UV and optical spectra by using state-of-the-art atmosphere models.
elements.sent-2411-058: Potsdam Wolf-Rayet (PoWR) atmosphere models \citep[][]{Grafener2002,Hamann2003,Sander2015 have proven to be ideal for analyzing massive stars \citep[e.g.,][]{Ramachandran2018,Ramachandran2019,Ramachandran2021,Pauli2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: We use these models to determine their main stellar parameters, such as the temperature of the star (\tstar), surface gravity (\logg), and luminosity (\lstar).
elements.sent-2411-060: We also determine wind parameters like the mass-loss rate ($\dot{M}$), terminal wind velocity (\vinf) and wind-momentum luminosity (\Dmom), as well as their chemical abundances (C, N, O), mechanical luminosity (\lmec), ionizing photons (\QH, \QHeI, and \QHeII), and X-rays.
elements.sent-2411-061: With additional standard tools, such as {\it iacob-broad \citep[][]{SimonDiaz2014 , we also determine projected rotational velocities (\vrot) as well as non-rotational broadening.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: With BONNSAI \citep[][]{Schneider2014 we report the predicted ages and evolutionary masses of the stars using stellar models from \citet[][]{Brott2011,Kohler2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: A comprehensive study of the massive stars in N11\,B, using the above-mentioned multi-wavelength observations and state-of-the-art analysis tools, is crucial for determining their physical parameters, quantifying their feedback contribution, and investigating the ecology of this environment.
elements.sent-2411-064: This article is structured as follows: in Sec.~\ref{Spectroscopic data we describe the spectroscopic observations of our stars; in Sec.~\ref{Analysis we model the observed multi-wavelength spectra with PoWR models and determine physical parameters; our results are discussed in Sec.~\ref{discussion .}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Finally, a summary and our conclusions are given in Sec.~\ref{conclusions .}

elements.sent-2411-082: In order to model the observed spectra of the stars, one needs to follow a sequence of steps: 1) determine the luminosity and extinction of the object, prioritizing recent Gaia DR3 photometry; 2) check for binarity, using available multi-epoch spectra; and 3) determine rotational and "macroturbulent" velocities, before fitting the spectral line widths.
elements.sent-2411-083: Once these steps are completed, step 4) is to model the spectra to obtain the physical parameters of the stars.
elements.sent-2411-085: Final stages include the determination of chemical abundances, X-ray luminosities, and refining the entire set of parameters comprehensively.
elements.sent-2411-086: Next we describe these different parts of our analysis.

elements.sent-2411-087: To determine the luminosity of our stars, we need to match the continuum of the synthetic spectrum of a selected model with the available photometry on the spectral energy distribution (SED).
elements.sent-2411-088: Reliable photometric values in the UV and optical ranges were taken from multiple references in the literature, from the U, B, V and R filters.
elements.sent-2411-089: Moreover, Gaia DR2 and DR3 values were included.
elements.sent-2411-090: In the NIR we use the J, H, and K bands.
elements.sent-2411-091: In Table~\ref{tab:photometry we list the photometric values from different references used in this work to construct the SEDs of the analyzed stars in N11\,B.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Although we make use of most of the values available from the literature, from UV up to the NIR, those values coming from Gaia \citep[][]{Gaia2018,Gaia2023 are prioritized in the optical range.}
elements.sent-2411-093: In the UV range, the calibrated fluxes from \hst\ \cosi\ and \stis\ can help as a photometric reference.
elements.sent-2411-094: Additionally, a careful inspection of each object was conducted using \hst/WFPC2 images in filters F656N and F502N, to ensure that we are considering the flux of a single object at the spatial resolution of \hst\ (with a pixel scale of 0.05~arcsec/pixel).
elements.sent-2411-095: However, we did not use these filters to obtain extra photometric values, since these bands correspond to \ha\ (F656N) and \oiiin\ (F502N), which are impacted by the nebular environment of the stars.
elements.sent-2411-096: We note that in the case of the spectra obtained with \xshooter, using slits of 0.7 and 0.8~arcsec $\times~11$~arcsec in the UVB and VIS ranges, respectively, we are observing single objects for PGMW\,3053, PGMW\,3061, PGMW\,3168, PGMW\,3100, PGMW\,3204, and PGMW\,3058.
elements.sent-2411-097: This is clearly not the case for PGMW\,3223, where at least a second object as bright as our target star is inside the apertures of \xshooter, and a third one (although dimmer) is inside the aperture of \fuse.
elements.sent-2411-098: Thus, care must be taken when interpreting these values in the SED.
elements.sent-2411-099: The same case for PGMW\,3120, which has three objects inside the \xshooter\ aperture, and two more (though dimmer) inside FUSE.
elements.sent-2411-100: On the other hand, \hst\ \cosi\ and \stis\ apertures only cover the star of interest, and for this reason their fluxes can be considered reliable references to construct their SEDs.

elements.sent-2411-102: Isocontours of brightness are used to inspect for probable contamination, like other spurious sources inside the slits covering our targets.
elements.sent-2411-103: Once these aspects were taken into consideration, the construction process of the SED is as follows: first, the flux-continuum of the model is scaled to the distance of the object.
elements.sent-2411-104: We assumed the same distance for all the objects, which is that of the LMC.
elements.sent-2411-105: Next, the reddening is determined by matching the slope of the SED, which is particularly sensitive towards the bluer wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-106: We take into account the extinction attributed to the Galactic foreground (\ebv$=0.04$ mag) and the LMC-law.
elements.sent-2411-107: For this purpose, we used the reddening laws by \citet[][]{Seaton1979 and \citet[][for the LMC]{Trundle2007 .}}

elements.sent-2411-111: When studying massive stars, one has to consider two possible scenarios for their evolution: the single \citep[][]{Conti1983 and the binary pathway \citep[e.g.,][]{Vanbeveren1997 .}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Since most of the massive stars are expected to be in a binary or multiple star system \citep[][]{Sana2012 , it is important to verify any indication of a binary companion before determining the stellar parameters as if we were dealing with a presumably single object.}
elements.sent-2411-113: In order to check for binarity, multi-epoch spectra are required.
elements.sent-2411-114: Our targets have multi-epoch observations from \giraffe.
elements.sent-2411-115: Although planned for other scientific cases, they allow us to check whether there is evidence of binarity, at least within the observed time frames, with the same resolution, exposure time, and SNR.
elements.sent-2411-116: The observed time periods span from 1~day to 1~month.
elements.sent-2411-117: However, the available observations are complex, and they need careful treatment for proper interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-118: For instance, the multi-epoch dates are different for each spectral range; e.g., it is of 45~days for the spectral range of $3850-4050$~\AA; 24~h for $4030-4200$~\AA; the same day for $4180-4400$~\AA; and same day for $4340-4340$~\AA, however different day from previous spectral range; 1~day for $4540-4760$~\AA\ and 31~days for $6300-6690$~\AA.
elements.sent-2411-119: Details are provided in Table~\ref{tab:spec_obs at the Appendix.}
elements.sent-2411-120: With this information, we checked for binary features with orbital periods matching the duration of the available observations.
elements.sent-2411-121: Here we check for any evidence of binarity in our sample.
elements.sent-2411-122: However, it is known that a star cannot be proven to be single.
elements.sent-2411-123: Even a lack of evidence of binarity does not necessarily mean it is not a binary.
elements.sent-2411-124: What we mean here by evidence of binarity includes radial velocity shifts for SB1 types, and variations in the line profile, e.g., double lines, for SB2 types.
elements.sent-2411-125: Also, the presence of a companion does not necessarily dominate the key features of the spectrum of the star, this would depend on its physical parameters.
elements.sent-2411-126: According to our analysis, PGMW\,3053 displays a variable \heiiwr\ profile within a 1-day time interval, as previously noted by \citet[][]{Evans2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-127: We attribute these features to wind variability, not to binarity.
elements.sent-2411-128: Other \heii\ lines do not show variability.
elements.sent-2411-129: PGMW\,3223 was found to be a binary.
elements.sent-2411-130: Its double line profile of \heiiwr\ could be interpreted as SB2 feature.
elements.sent-2411-131: However, no other \heii\ lines display this characteristic feature.

elements.sent-2411-136: In order to determine the projected rotational velocity (\vrot) of the stars, one ought to measure the broadening of their photospheric lines in absorption.
elements.sent-2411-137: Unlike the Balmer and He lines, the broadening of metal lines is normally attributed to macroturbulence rather than pressure.
elements.sent-2411-138: However, metal lines are generally weaker than H and He lines, or even absent, when the SNR is not sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-139: Thus, dedicated observations should be analyzed when determining this parameter.
elements.sent-2411-140: Here we use {\it iacob-broad \citep[][]{SimonDiaz2014 , a standard semi-automatized tool to measure \vrot\ in OB stars \citep[e.g.,][]{Holgado2018,Berlanas2020 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: This tool employs the Fourier transform (FT) and goodness-of-fit (GOF) methods.
elements.sent-2411-142: Basically, the model spectra are convolved to the instrumental resolution of the analyzed spectra, and the rotational velocities are obtained.
elements.sent-2411-143: The values we report are the current rotational velocities at the equator, times the sine of the inclination towards the observer.
elements.sent-2411-144: The inclination is an unknown parameter with the available data.
elements.sent-2411-145: As an example, in Fig.~\ref{fig:vrot_n11-18 we show the graphical output obtained from the {\it iacob-broad tool for a particular metal line in absorption of the PGMW\,3053 star.}}
elements.sent-2411-146: The plot is rich in information.
elements.sent-2411-147: Basically, the line profile of the selected metal, in this case \niiia\ (in the upper left), is shown with different fittings from the two different methods: 1) FT, and 2) GOF with their projections, for "macroturbulent" velocity (\vmac) to the left, and for the rotational component (\vrot) to the top of the plot.
elements.sent-2411-148: There are four different fittings indicated with different colours: 1) red indicates the \vrot\ corresponding to the first zero of the FT; 2) blue for \vrot\ and \vmac\ obtained from GOF; 3) green indicates the \vrot\ from the GOF, if \vmac\ is assumed to be zero; and 4), purple shows the fitting of the \vmac\ from the GOF, assuming \vrot\ is the first zero of the FT.
elements.sent-2411-149: We select the values from the last assumption, given it provides the best fit of the observed line, considering both \vmac\ and \vrot\ contributions simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-150: We use exclusively metal lines in absorption to determine their \vrot\ broadening component.
elements.sent-2411-151: The metal lines we use are \niiia\ (for PGMW\,3053 and PGMW\,3058).
elements.sent-2411-152: When \niii\ lines are too weak or absent, then we check for more intense, and resolved lines, present in the spectrum of a particular star under analysis.
elements.sent-2411-153:
We use higher ionizing state N lines, though weaker, like 
va\ and 
vb, when 
iii\ is not detected (e.g., in PGMW\,3061).
elements.sent-2411-154: Other metal lines are also used as well.
elements.sent-2411-155: We used \siiva\ (for PGMW\,3168) and \civa\ (for PGMW\,3100, PGMW\,3120 and PGMW\,3204).
elements.sent-2411-156:
When using 
iii\ to determine rotation, we prioritize 
iiia\ over 
iiib, given the latter is usually weaker and more difficult to fit.
elements.sent-2411-157: The same case applies for \siiv; \siiva\ is usually stronger than \siivb, and sometimes \siivb\ is even in emission.
elements.sent-2411-158: We do not use lines in emission to determine \vrot.
elements.sent-2411-159: Carbon lines also help, in the case of \civ, \civa\ is prioritized over \civb, which is usually weaker.
elements.sent-2411-160: Oxygen lines like \oiii\,$\lambda$5592.3 also helps (e.g., for PGMW\,3223).
elements.sent-2411-161: We report our results for \vrot\ in Table~\ref{tab:results .}
elements.sent-2411-162: The {\it iacob-broad tool provides computational errors for \vrot\ and \vmac\ of around $5\%$.}
elements.sent-2411-163: However, we assumed a value of $20\%$ to account for errors associated with the methodology.
elements.sent-2411-164: This represents the lower error, and the upper error is consistent with the difference between \vrot\ (GOF) and \vrot\ (FT).
elements.sent-2411-165: Detailed information, like EW and SNR, for the selected lines of each star is given in Sec.~\ref{comments .}

elements.sent-2411-166: We remark that the {\it iacob-broad tool determines the broadening of a given photosperic line, separating the contribution from two different effects: rotational broadening and non-rotational broadening.}
elements.sent-2411-168: On the other hand, non-rotational broadening, generally assumed to be macroturbulent broadening, is less understood.
elements.sent-2411-169: In fact, the given name is not necessarily related with the physical meaning of term "macroturbulence", which \citet[][]{SimonDiaz2017 explain how it was originally introduced in the framework of cool stars, and it is defined as "large-turbulent motions of material in the line-forming region" in that context.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Here we aim to measure \vrot\ in order to study an invoked correlation between this parameter and the chemical enrichment of the stars (see Sec.~\ref{abundances ).}
elements.sent-2411-171: However, we also report the non-rotational broadening, refereed here as \vmac\ for simplicity, because this broadening effect is important.
elements.sent-2411-172: Its contribution may even exceed the rotational velocity term \citep[see e.g.,][]{SimonDiaz2017, Holgado2018 , and therefore it ought to be considered in the modeling of the spectra, independently of its physical meaning.}
elements.sent-2411-173: Otherwise, if assuming \vmac$=0$~\kms, one could be overestimating \vrot.
elements.sent-2411-174: Discussing the physical origin of the non-rotational broadening in our stars is beyond the scope of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-175: However, we refer the reader to the detailed study by \citet[][]{SimonDiaz2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-176: Basically, they conclude that the so-called macroturbulence can be attributed to "pulsational modes linked to a heat-driven mechanism" and/or "cyclic surface motions caused by turbulent pressure instabilities in subsurface convection zones".
elements.sent-2411-177: Most of our stars have an important "macroturbulent" contribution which has to be considered in our modeling.
elements.sent-2411-178: Reporting this value is also helpful for reproducibility purposes and future reference.
elements.sent-2411-179: The \vmac/\vrot\ ratios we find here are consistent with the findings by \citet[][]{SimonDiaz2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-180: Finally, we also determine the \vrot\ parameter using the \hei\ and \heii\ lines.
elements.sent-2411-181: However, even for the same star, we obtain different values from those obtained with metal lines.
elements.sent-2411-182: This is not surprising, because as previously mentioned, other broadening (no-rotational) mechanisms start to play a role.
elements.sent-2411-183: Thus, we avoid using these lines to determine \vrot.
elements.sent-2411-184: Only in cases where no metal lines are present, H and He could be considered for a rough estimation of rotational broadening.
elements.sent-2411-185: Once we determined key parameters like the \lstar, \ebv, \vrot, and checked for binarity in each of our objects, then we can proceed with the modeling of the observed spectra.
elements.sent-2411-186: We started by using synthetic spectra from the OB model grids by \citet[][]{Hainich2019 , generated with the PoWR model atmosphere program\footnote{\url{https://www.astro.physik.uni-potsdam.de/PoWR/ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: PoWR models are basically the simulated emergent spectra of hot stars with specified stellar and wind parameters, which consider non-local thermodynamic equilibrium (Non-LTE) radiative transfer, spherical symmetry, stationary outflow, metal line blanketing and wind inhomogeneities.
elements.sent-2411-188: The rate equations for statistical equilibrium and radiative transfer are solved simultaneously by the PoWR code in the co-moving frame and secure energy conservation \citep[see][for more details]{Grafener2002,Hamann2003,Sander2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-189: Next, we calculated new synthetic spectra to model the stellar and wind parameters of our targets.
elements.sent-2411-190: The basic input parameters of a specific stellar model are the bolometric luminosity \lstar\ and the stellar temperature \tstar.
elements.sent-2411-191: These are related to the stellar radius \rstar\ via the Stefan-Boltzmann equation.
elements.sent-2411-192: The stellar radius is defined at a Rosseland-mean optical depth of 20.
elements.sent-2411-193: While \tstar\ is the effective temperature related to that radius, one can also define an effective temperature \teff\ or T2/3, which is related to the radius where the Rosseland-mean optical depth reaches 2/3.
elements.sent-2411-194: For the type of atmospheres considered in this paper, the difference between \tstar\ and \teff\ is negligibly small.
elements.sent-2411-195: Further fundamental parameters of a model include the surface gravity \logg, the abundances, and the wind parameters (mass-loss rate $\dot{M}$ and terminal wind velocity $v_\infty$).
elements.sent-2411-196: The modeling procedure is described in Sec.~\ref{Fitting .}
elements.sent-2411-197: For establishing the non-LTE population numbers, the radiative transfer is calculated assuming the same Gaussian profiles in the absorption coefficient, with the width set equivalent to a Doppler velocity of 30~\kms.
elements.sent-2411-198: This is a usual procedure and should account for turbulence, pressure broadening, and multiplet splitting of lines, which are not accounted for at this stage.
elements.sent-2411-199: For the hydrostatic equation in the quasi-static part of the atmosphere, we adopt a turbulence pressure (\vmic) corresponding to 20~\kms\ \citep{Shenar2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-200: When the model stratification has been established, the emergent spectrum is calculated in the observer's frame of reference.
elements.sent-2411-201: Here, detailed line broadening is taken into account, and multiplets are split into their components.
elements.sent-2411-202: A microturbulence velocity ($\xi$) of 14~\kms\ is found to give adequate results \citep[e.g.,][]{Hainich2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-203: In the wind, we tentatively adopt an additional contribution to $\xi$ as $10\%$ of the local wind speed.
elements.sent-2411-204: We used a clumping factor, or density contrast (D) of 10 (filling factor $f_{V =0.1$), as described in \citet[][]{Hamann1998 .}}
elements.sent-2411-205: We assumed a wind velocity field following the $\beta$-law from \citet[][]{Castor1975 , with the exponent $\beta=0.8$, which is a typical value for O-type stars \citep[e.g.,][]{Kudritzki1989, Repolust2004 .}}
elements.sent-2411-206: We included the following atoms in our analysis: H, He, C, N, O, Mg, Si, P, S, Fe, and Ni.
elements.sent-2411-207: The mass fractions for these elements are adopted from \citet[][for the LMC]{Trundle2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-208: The H ($X_{\rm H}$) and He ($X_{\rm He}$) mass fractions are set to 0.738 and 0.258, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-209: The total CNO value is conserved, unless otherwise specified.
elements.sent-2411-210: When certain spectra need a different abundance (e.g., N enrichment), then the relative values of these elements are changed accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-211: We start with typical mass fractions for CNO elements of: $X_{\rm C =4.7\times10^{-4}$, $X_{\rm N =7.8\times10^{-5}$, and $X_{\rm O =2.6\times10^{-3}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-212: The values for magnesium and silicon are $X_{\rm Mg =2.1\times10^{-4}$ and $X_{\rm Si =3.2\times10^{-4}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Since there are not direct abundance measurements of phosphorus in the LMC \citep[see e.g.,][]{Massa2003 , P is set to 0.5~\zsun, assuming a solar abundance from \citet[][]{Asplund2009 .}}
elements.sent-2411-215: The values for P and S are set to: $X_{\rm P =2.9\times10^{-6}$ and $X_{\rm S =1.5\times10^{-4}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-216: The iron group elements (from Sc to Ni) are treated with the super-level approach from \citet[][]{Grafener2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-217: For Fe, the adopted value was $X_{\rm Fe =7.0\times10^{-4}$.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Dielectronic recombination and autoionization mechanisms were taken into account for CNO ions.
elements.sent-2411-219: PoWR can also consider embedded X-rays, and they were included to model UV features.

elements.sent-2411-220: Although the stellar and wind parameters of the O-type stars can be determined by specific diagnostic lines separately, it is important to note that these are not independent of each other, and thus all the lines of the spectrum, from the UV to the optical, should be addressed comprehensively.
elements.sent-2411-221: In this work, we used multi-wavelength observations to model the sample of ULLYSES targets in N11\,B.
elements.sent-2411-222: We analyse spectra from \fuse/MRDS, \hst/\stis, \hst/\cosi\ in the UV, and from VLT/\xshooter\ in the optical.
elements.sent-2411-223: Given the multi-wavelength spectral coverage, along with information on distance, precise photometric magnitudes, radial velocities, projected rotation, and extinction, we can construct the SED of each star and determine their bolometric luminosity.
elements.sent-2411-224: Next, we need to model the spectra of the stars.
elements.sent-2411-226: First, we chose a synthetic spectra from the grid of PoWR models in the \tstar-\logg\ plane by \citet[][]{Hainich2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-227: Then, we chose the one with the closest \tstar\ and \logg\ to our spectra.
elements.sent-2411-228: Afterward, specific lines and their profiles are carefully examined to recalculate new models and better model the key lines for each physical attribute.
elements.sent-2411-230: Next, we explain the determination of each parameter.
elements.sent-2411-231: The effective temperature (\tstar) of the stars is determined by modeling the He lines (e.g., \hei\,$\lambda\lambda$3819.6, 4120.8, 4471.5, 4713.1, 5015.7, 5875.6, 6678.1, 7065.2, and \heii\,$\lambda\lambda$3813.5, 4200.0, 4541.6, 5411.5, 6074.2, 6118.3, 6170.7, 6233.8, 6310.8, 6406.4, 6528.0, 6685.0, 6890.9, 7177.5, 7592.7), specifically the \hei\ and \heii\ ratio.
elements.sent-2411-232: The pairs of \hei\,$\lambda$3819.6 with \heii\,$\lambda$3813.5, and \hei\,$\lambda$4471.5 with \heii\,$\lambda$4541.6 are particularly useful for this diagnostic.
elements.sent-2411-233: Except for \heiiwr, which is known to be mostly sensitive to the strength of the stellar winds of the stars.
elements.sent-2411-234: Singlet lines of \hei\ (1s2p P), at 4387.9 and 4921.9~\AA, have shown to be problematic in previous studies \citep[see e.g.,][]{Najarro2006 , and the reason is still unclear in the community.}
elements.sent-2411-235: Thus, these lines were not considered for \tstar\ determination.
elements.sent-2411-236: The gravitational acceleration at the surface of the stars (\logg), can in principle be determined by modeling the wings of the H-Balmer lines, since they are expected to be pressure-broadened by the Stark effect.
elements.sent-2411-237: However, care must be taken with \ha, since it is also sensitive to stellar winds.
elements.sent-2411-238: Therefore, mainly the lines of \hb, \hg, \hd, \hep, and H9--H17, were considered to determine this parameter.
elements.sent-2411-239: The rotational and non-rotational broadening determined from the metal lines present in the spectrum (see Sec.~\ref{Rotation ) must be considered before determining the surface gravity; otherwise, \logg\ risks overestimation.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Since \tstar\ and \logg\ are intrinsically correlated parameters and cannot be determined independently, they ought to be modeled simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-241: The main diagnostic lines for the wind mass-loss rate ($\dot{M}$), in the optical range, are typically \ha\ and \heiiwr.
elements.sent-2411-242: In the UV range \nv\,$\lambda\lambda$1238.8, 1242.8 and \civ\,$\lambda\lambda$1548.2, 1550.8 are particularly sensible to the wind.
elements.sent-2411-243: The terminal wind velocity (\vinf) is determined from the blue edge of the \civ\,$\lambda$1548.2 in absorption.
elements.sent-2411-244: The obtained parameters of the stars are given in Table~\ref{tab:results1 .}
elements.sent-2411-245: We initially assumed standard LMC abundance values of C, N and O.
elements.sent-2411-246: However, we need to change these values to better model certain metal lines.
elements.sent-2411-247: The abundances of the rest of the elements are, in principle, fixed for all the stars.

elements.sent-2411-584: We investigated in detail the stellar parameters, wind features, mechanical, and ionizing feedback of eight ULLYSES targets: benchmark O-type stars with spectral types ranging from O2 to O8 and with different luminosity classes, in the N11\,B star-forming region in the LMC.
elements.sent-2411-585: State-of-the-art PoWR models and other standard analysis tools were used in a homogeneous approach.
elements.sent-2411-586: Novel UV \hst/\stis\ and \cosi\ high-quality spectra from the ULLYSES project, as well as optical spectra from \xshooter\ at VLT, were analyzed, along with the most recent Gaia DR3 photometry.
elements.sent-2411-587: Next, we summarize our main findings.
elements.sent-2411-588: We report ages between $2-4.5$~Myr and masses of $30-60$\Msun\ for the O-type stars analyzed in N11\,B.
elements.sent-2411-589: Such young ages are consistent with the absence of WR stars, the fact that no SNe have been reported in this region yet, and with the hypothesis of a sequential star formation scenario from the center of N11 to the surrounding regions.
elements.sent-2411-590: We show that the \mev\ and \mspec\ do not follow a clear one-to-one relation.
elements.sent-2411-591: This mass discrepancy is consistent with previous findings in the literature \citep[e.g.,][in the Magellanic Clouds]{Herrero1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-592: The analyzed O-type stars follow a wind-momentum luminosity relation, which is consistent with previously reported observational relations and with the theoretically predicted relation by \citet{Vink2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-593: We investigated whether nitrogen enrichment correlates with \vrot.
elements.sent-2411-594: The O-type stars analyzed here have rotational velocities ranging from 55 to 143~\kms, with a median value of 100~\kms.
elements.sent-2411-596: We observe nitrogen enrichment in most of the stars, up to a factor of 7.
elements.sent-2411-597: However, we do not find a trend with \vrot, challenging the hypothesis that nitrogen enrichment is correlated with rotation in massive stars.
elements.sent-2411-598: The effect of 'macroturbulence' on line broadening was found to be non-negligible.
elements.sent-2411-599: On the contrary, \vmac\ is an important contributor and in most cases, it even dominates the line broadening.
elements.sent-2411-600: Our result is consistent with previous findings of \citep[e.g.,][]{SimonDiaz2017, Holgado2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-601: We recommend not ignoring its contribution to the total broadening of a line, and avoiding the use of non-metallic lines, when possible, for determining \vrot.
elements.sent-2411-602: Additionally, we find that even for the same star, the metal lines give different values compared to those obtained with the Balmer and He lines.
elements.sent-2411-603: Therefore, these lines should be avoided when possible.
elements.sent-2411-604: In the UV range, we observed that reducing the phosphorus abundances by a factor of four results in the modeled \pv\ lines matching the observed lines, similar to the well-studied macroclumping effect.
elements.sent-2411-605: By including X-rays in our PoWR models, we quantified the amount of X-rays required to reproduce important UV spectral features in our stars.
elements.sent-2411-606: We independently report $L_\mathrm{X /L_\mathrm{bol}$ ratios, between $-7.5$ to $-6.6$, including for the first time the values for four O-type stars in N11\,B.}
elements.sent-2411-607: These values are consistent with soft X-ray emitters and previous findings \citep[][]{Naze2014, Crowther2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-608: The fact that N11\,B is free of exotic objects gives us the opportunity to study the ionizing feedback in a star-forming region exclusively through its O-type stars.
elements.sent-2411-609: We report total ionizing fluxes of $\log(\sum$\QH$)=50.5$~ph\,s$^{-1}$, $\log(\sum$\QHeI$)=49.6$~ph\,s$^{-1}$, and $\log(\sum$\QHeII$)=44.4$~ph\,s$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-610: If X-rays are considered, then $\log(\sum$\QHeII$)=44.8$ph\,s$^{-1}$, which represents an additional 0.4~dex of ionization.
elements.sent-2411-611: The total mechanical luminosity of the eight analyzed stars is $\sum$\,\lmec$=2900$~\lsun.
elements.sent-2411-612: PGMW\,3061, the earliest analyzed star (ON2\,III(f*)), is the main contributor with $\log$ (\lmec/\lsun)$=3.0$.
elements.sent-2411-613: It is a factor of $3-14$ stronger in \lmec\ compared to the next brightest and faintest sources analyzed, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-614: PGMW\,3061 is also the main contributor to \QH\ and \QHeI.
elements.sent-2411-615: PGMW\,3058, an O3V((f*)) star, is the main contributor to \QHeII.
elements.sent-2411-616: Given the absence of WR stars and SNe remnants, the O-type stars alone provide the feedback in N11\,B.
elements.sent-2411-617: Summarizing, this work is part of a larger project aimed at determining the stellar and wind parameters of the OB stars in the ULLYSES sample.
elements.sent-2411-618: This study is among the initial steps in using PoWR models to achieve this goal in a homogeneous way.
elements.sent-2411-619: The next step is to increase the sample size analyzed with PoWR and statistically compare the results with theoretical predictions.
elements.sent-2411-620: Here, we presented the methodology required for this, including the analysis of novel UV spectra where key wind parameters are found, along with other features to constrain the stellar properties, particularly more reliable luminosities and extinction of the stars with UV fluxes and Gaia photometry.

elements.sent-2411-637: The search for binarity features in the analyzed stars in N11\,B is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:n11-38_giraffe to Fig.~\ref{fig:n11-31_giraffe .}}
elements.figure-2411-043: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[trim={1.0cm 2.3cm 1.0cm 2.1cm},clip,width=0.43\linewidth]{n11-38_giraffe_1.pdf}~ \includegraphics[trim={1.0cm 2.3cm 1.0cm 2.1cm},clip,width=0.43\linewidth]{n11-38_giraffe_2.pdf} \par\end{centering} \caption{ PGMW\,3100 \giraffe\ multi-epoch spectra to check for binariry. Only \ha\ displays a variable profile, probably because of wind variability. Some of the lines have been shifted by the difference in radial velocity, $<9$~\kms\ (green line), to compare their line profiles. } \label{fig:n11-38_giraffe}
elements.figure-2411-044: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[trim={1.0cm 2.3cm 1.0cm 2.1cm},clip,width=0.43\linewidth]{n11-13_giraffe_1.pdf}~ \includegraphics[trim={1.0cm 2.3cm 1.0cm 2.1cm},clip,width=0.43\linewidth]{n11-13_giraffe_2.pdf} \par\end{centering} \caption{ PGMW\,3223 \giraffe\ multi-epoch spectra to check for binariry: radial velocity or line profile difference. Some of the lines have been shifted by the difference in radial velocity, $20-30$~\kms\ (green line), to compare their line profiles. } \label{fig:n11-13_giraffe}
elements.figure-2411-045: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[trim={1.0cm 2.3cm 1.0cm 2.1cm},clip,width=0.9\linewidth]{n11-48_giraffe_1.pdf}~ \par\end{centering} \caption{ PGMW\,3204 \giraffe\ multi-epoch spectra to check for binariry: radial velocity or line profile difference. No evidence for binary component in the Balmer nor He lines. } \label{fig:n11-48_giraffe1}
elements.figure-2411-046: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[trim={1.0cm 2.3cm 1.0cm 2.1cm},clip,width=0.9\linewidth]{n11-48_giraffe_2.pdf}~ \par\end{centering} \caption{ PGMW\,3204 \giraffe\ multi-epoch spectra to check for binariry: radial velocity or line profile difference. elements.section-2411-010: Additional O-type stars of diffrerent sub-type in LMC
elements.sent-2411-638: The physical parameters obtained with PoWR for three additional O-type stars of different sub-types in LMC are reported in Table~\ref{tab:results4 .}
